title: Dam Square Origins
route_description: Follow Amsterdam’s waterways to see how water control became trade power—and how infrastructure quietly shaped the city’s identity.
summary_explorer: This square began as a dam in the river Amstel—a simple structure that sparked a city.
summary_deepdive: The original dam controlled water flow and enabled trade, anchoring early settlement and market life around reliability and access.
summary_bts: Hydraulic infrastructure is governance: controlling water shaped taxation, commerce, and long-term planning capacity.
what_to_notice: Notice how infrastructure becomes a social magnet.
did_you_know: The word Amsterdam comes from the Amstel dam.

title: Oude Kerk
route_description: Follow Amsterdam’s waterways to see how water control became trade power—and how infrastructure quietly shaped the city’s identity.
summary_explorer: The city’s oldest building watched merchants and sailors pass.
summary_deepdive: Churches organized time, identity, and social stability in trade cities.
summary_bts: Religious institutions were administrative infrastructure tied to civic authority.
what_to_notice: Look for how sacred spaces anchor civic life.
did_you_know: It sits today in the Red Light District—layers of meaning coexist.

title: VOC Headquarters Area
route_description: Follow Amsterdam’s waterways to see how water control became trade power—and how infrastructure quietly shaped the city’s identity.
summary_explorer: Ships sailed from here toward Asia—and wealth returned.
summary_deepdive: The VOC combined maritime logistics, finance, and imperial networks.
summary_bts: Joint-stock governance and risk pooling shaped modern corporate structures.
what_to_notice: Trade relies on systems like contracts and accounting.
did_you_know: Often described as the first multinational corporation.
